full_response:
Let  

ρ ≡ m / V … “density’’ that was actually measured in some inertial frame S.  
(Here m is the total rest-mass contained in the stick and V is the spatial volume of the stick defined by the set of points that are simultaneous in S.)

Step 1: Pick a convenient frame  
Take S₀ to be the rest frame of the meter stick.  In S₀

• its proper length is L₀ = 1 m,  
• its proper cross–sectional area A ( ⟂ to the motion we shall later consider) is unchanged by boosts,  
• its proper volume is therefore  
 V₀ = A L₀ ,  
• its rest mass is m (a Lorentz scalar).  
The proper (rest-frame) density is  

 ρ₀ = m / V₀ .

Step 2: Transform to another inertial frame S′ that moves with speed v = βc along the axis of the stick  

(i) Volume seen in S′  

Only the dimension parallel to v contracts. Hence  

 L′ = L₀ / γ  (γ ≡ 1/√(1–β²))  

and the volume becomes  

 V′ = A L′ = V₀ / γ .

(ii) Mass seen in S′  

The invariant quantity is the rest mass m, so observers in S′ still assign the value m to the stick’s invariant mass.  (If one insists on using the obsolete concept of “relativistic mass’’ m_rel = γm, the conclusion below will be modified by an extra factor γ, but the central point—non-invariance—remains the same.)

Step 3: Density seen in S′  

 ρ′ = m / V′ = m /(V₀/γ) = γ ρ₀ .

Thus ρ′ ≠ ρ₀ as long as v ≠ 0.  More generally, for two arbitrary inertial frames S and S′ moving at relative speed v parallel to the stick,

 ρ′ = γ ρ ,

so different observers do not agree on the value of the ordinary three–density.

Step 4: Why the disagreement is inevitable  

• Volume is a purely spatial quantity; under a Lorentz transformation the slice of space-time labeled “t = const’’ in one frame is not the same slice labeled “t′ = const’’ in another.  
• Mass (rest mass) is invariant, so the ratio m / V mixes a scalar with a frame–dependent spatial measure; therefore it cannot be invariant.  
• The covariant object is the number (or mass) 4-current  
 J^μ = ρ₀ U^μ ;  
its time component in any frame gives the density that observers in that frame will measure.  Different frames pick out different components of J^μ and therefore obtain different numeric densities.

Conclusion  
Observers in different inertial frames will not in general agree on the density of a meter stick; the density measured in a frame moving at speed v relative to the stick is larger by a factor γ = 1/√(1–v²/c²) than the proper density measured in the stick’s rest frame.

\boxed{\text{No. Density is not Lorentz invariant; a frame moving at speed }v\text{ parallel to the stick sees } \rho'=\gamma\,\rho\ .}

Confidence: 84%
extracted_answer: \text{No. Density is not Lorentz invariant; a frame moving at speed }v\text{ parallel to the stick sees } \rho'=\gamma\,\rho\ .
